summary: Jeff Atwood reflects on his father's unfulfilling career journey and his own years of drifting between jobs before realizing his true passion was software development. He argues that developers should actively choose employers who share their passion rather than letting happenstance determine their careers. The post critiques companies that treat software as a cost center, noting that such organizations drive away talented developers and retain only those who clock in for a paycheck. Atwood advocates for working at companies where software engineering is respected and where the work is genuinely enjoyable. He concludes by urging developers to take advantage of the tech boom to find fulfilling positions at passion-driven companies.
key_points: Self-awareness about what you truly love doing is surprisingly difficult but essential for career satisfaction
Companies that treat developers as commodities will only retain average talent while passionate developers leave
Developers should actively select companies rather than passively accepting whatever opportunities arise
Working with people who share your passion for software fundamentally changes the work experience
The tech boom provides an opportunity to be selective and find genuinely fulfilling work
money_quotes: Self-awareness is a perplexingly difficult thing from here on the inside.
Life is too short to stay at a job where you're not doing the things you want to do, where you're not enjoying yourself.
Enterprises that consider developers 'commodities and low level craftsmen' are doomed to have (at best) average developers working for them.
Companies would no longer be able to select me from a generic lineup of candidates. Instead, I would select companies.
I will choose where I want to work.
The main reason I had to start this company was to have fun at work.
You deserve to work at a company where software engineering is respected.
This stuff is supposed to be fun.
key_insight: Developers should take control of their careers by actively choosing employers who share their passion for software rather than drifting between jobs that treat development as a cost center.
